title: validateProject invariants are hardcoded (completed-task-verification, decision-forcing-artifact) — DEC-0025 violation; must be config-declared
description: validateProject (project-sdk.ts ~860-975, machinery unit 2.1 / commit 73e568d) hardcodes two substrate invariants: (1) completed-task-has-verification — every task status=completed requires ≥1 verification_verifies_item edge (child=task); (2) decision-cites-forcing-artifact — every decision requires ≥1 decision_addresses_{issue,feature,gap} edge (parent=decision). These name specific block kinds (tasks, decisions), a specific status (completed), and specific relation_type canonical_ids — all hardcoded in the validator. Per DEC-0025 (substrate canon is vocabulary-neutral; a user's block/relation/status conception bootstraps entirely from config + schemas + macros without code changes), a user adopting the framework with a different conception (decisions that don't need forcing artifacts; tasks that don't need verification; entirely different block kinds) is forced into pi-context's specific invariants, unconfigurably. Surfaced during machinery unit 2.1: the unit-2.1 guard-flip fix (relations.length>0 → if config) extended these hardcoded invariants from dormant-on-zero-edge to firing on EVERY config-present substrate, sharpening the latent DEC-0025 violation. The invariants must be CONFIG-DECLARED (config.invariants[]) + validateProject must consume them generically (no hardcoded block/status/relation literals). Design established at analysis/2026-05-17-config-declared-invariants-design.md.

evidence.reference: validateProject hardcoded invariant 1 (completed-task-verification) ~928-945 + invariant 2 (decision-forcing-artifact) ~947-975 — block/status/relation_type literals hardcoded
DEC-0025 vocabulary-neutral substrate canon — source ships zero vocabulary commitment; invariants must be config-declared
config has no invariants[] declaration surface; lens 'where' field-equality (lines 124-130) is the reusable predicate precedent
established design: config.invariants[] requires-edge class + generic consumer + field-equality where + config-data defaults

impact: validateProject enforces pi-context-specific invariants on every config-present substrate. A vocabulary-neutral adopter (DEC-0025) with a different conception cannot escape the decision-forcing-artifact + completed-task-verification rules without modifying source. Blocks .context re-derivation (DEC-0036): re-population satisfies invariants at authoring time + needs config.invariants[] declared before validateProject gates it — if invariants stay hardcoded, the re-populated .context is forced into pi-context's specific invariants rather than its own declared ones. Sequencing: this fix must land before .context re-population.
proposed_resolution: Per analysis/2026-05-17-config-declared-invariants-design.md (all 3 flagged forks resolved as derivable: requires-edge-class-only [anti-over-engineering/YAGNI]; scaffold-placed config-data defaults [via-negativa + DEC-0025]; error severity default [preserve current behavior]): (1) add config.invariants[] to config.schema.json — requires-edge class {id, class, block, where?:{field:value}, relation_types[], direction:as_parent|as_child, severity?, message?}; (2) ConfigBlock type + loadConfig carry invariants; (3) validateProject replaces the 2 hardcoded invariant blocks with a generic loop over config.invariants ?? [] (zero hardcoded block/status/relation literals; reuses idIndex + relations; field-equality where predicate reusing the lens-composition precedent, NOT expression.ts — pi-context has no dep on pi-workflows expression.ts, importing inverts layering); (4) declare the 2 canonical invariants in .project/config.json + the /context-init scaffold default config (config-data, user-overridable; source ships zero invariant vocabulary → DEC-0025-clean); (5) tests: generic consumer fires per declared invariant incl. ABSENCE case (missing required edge → error) per the unit-2.1 false-pass lesson. Lands as a machinery unit before .context re-population (DEC-0036 step 3).
